# Plan: AGENTS.md Review Fixes + TestHarness Integration

## Context
Reviewer suggestions on the `agentic_hackathon` branch require updates to the agent governance file and integration of the hit-format hook test into the TMAP8 TestHarness so it runs via `./run_tests`.

---

## Changes

### 1. Rename `tmap8_agents.MD` → `AGENTS.md`
Simple rename. Claude Code (and Codex) automatically read `AGENTS.md` at session start.
```
git mv tmap8_agents.MD AGENTS.md
```

### 2. AGENTS.md edits (5 targeted changes)

**a) Session log — generalize jsonl path, handle .claude vs .codex**

Replace machine-specific path with a general description:
> Before committing, copy the current session's `.jsonl` file into `doc/content/agent/<issue-number>/`. For Claude Code sessions the file is the most recently modified `.jsonl` under `~/.claude/projects/<repo-slug>/`; for Codex sessions check the equivalent Codex session directory. Do not remove old session files already in that directory — append the new one alongside them.

**b) Remove units comment requirement**

Current: *"Write parameter values in the units the reference source used; convert explicitly using the MOOSE units system with the conversion documented in a comment"*

Replace with: *"Write parameter values in the units the reference source used; use the MOOSE units system for conversion — the units system is self-documenting and does not require inline comments"*

**c) Keep old session files**

Add to the jsonl committing bullet: *"Do not remove session files already present in that directory — if multiple Claude/Codex sessions contribute to the same branch and issue, all session files must be kept."*

**d) Replace "MOOSE test system test" → "MOOSE TestHarness test"**

In C++ code section: *"Every new class requires a MOOSE TestHarness test in `test/tests/` before committing"*

**e) SQA issue template note — already done; no change needed**

---

```
git mv scripts/test-hit-format-hook.sh test/tests/agent_tests/test-hit-format-hook.sh

`RunCommand` type; command is relative to the test directory so no path prefix needed:
```

The hook script references `scripts/test-hit-format-hook.sh` only indirectly (it's a separate test utility), so no changes needed there.

# AGENTS.md

## Environment
- Use the MOOSE conda environment (`conda activate moose`)
- If `./tmap8-opt` or `run_tests` throws an error, stop and ask the user
- Pre-commit hooks handle all formatting and input file validation on every `git commit` — do not invoke `clang-format`, `black`, or `hit format` manually
- `moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit check <file>.i` is available to validate input files during development — use it before committing

## Before writing any code or input file
- Browse `doc/content/` to understand documentation standards and existing patterns before starting work
- Before writing a new C++ class, look through existing MOOSE classes in the codebase to confirm expected syntax and patterns
- Documentation in `doc/content/` may not accurately reflect current code behavior — once you identify a class to use in an input file, find its C++ implementation and read it to confirm the class does what you expect before writing the input file

## C++ code
- Every new class requires a MOOSE test system test in `test/tests/` before committing
- Every new class requires a MooseDocs page following existing TMAP8 doc structure
- Never hardcode physical parameters — first check if it is already defined in `doc/content/source/utils/TMAP8PhysicalConstants`, and if not available, expose everything through `addParam`
- Code is unit-agnostic; units live in input files only

## Input files
- Before writing an input file, look through existing MOOSE and TMAP8 classes to confirm object names and parameter syntax
- Use `moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit check <file>.i` to validate before committing
- Use `./tmap8-opt -i <file>.i` to run input files
- Write parameter values in the units the reference source used; convert explicitly using the MOOSE units system with the conversion documented in a comment
- State the unit system at the top of every input file in a comment block

## Testing
- Run tests only when `.C`, `.h`, or `test/tests/` files change
- Run only the affected module: `./run_tests -j<N> --re <module>`

## Session log
- Every session must be associated with a GitHub issue on `idaholab/TMAP8`; ask the user for the issue number at the start of the session if one is not provided, or create one if the user instructs you to
- **SQA requirement:** When creating a GitHub issue with `gh`, you must use the appropriate template from `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`; read the available templates first, select the one that fits (e.g. `bug-report.md` for bugs, `feature-request.md` for new features or tasks), and populate all sections in the template body — passing a bare description without following the template structure is not acceptable
- Before committing, copy the raw `.jsonl` session file for the current session into `doc/content/agent/<issue-number>/`; the session file is the most recently modified `.jsonl` under `~/.claude/projects/-Users-bhavcv-projects-TMAP8/`

## Committing
- Commit at minimum after: every input file that successfully runs, every test that passes or fails, and every new class added
- If a test or run failed, commit the failure state first (include the full error message in the commit body), then commit the fix separately
- Every commit must reference the associated GitHub issue (e.g. `Refs #<issue-number>` or `Closes #<issue-number>` as appropriate) in the commit message
- **Every commit must include an updated copy of the session `.jsonl`** — before staging, copy the current session file from `~/.claude/projects/-Users-bhavcv-projects-TMAP8/<session-id>.jsonl` to `doc/content/agent/<issue-number>/` and stage it; this is a critical SQA requirement
- Every commit must include these trailers:
```
Refs #<issue-number>
AI-assisted: true
AI-model: <model name>
AI-version: <model version>
Co-Authored-By: <model name> <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

## Pull requests
- PR description must include: physics/modeling assumptions made, AI reasoning log (decision points and alternatives considered), and tests added
- Never merge autonomously

## Never do
- Modify existing passing tests without explicit user instruction
- Modify the build system without explicit user instruction
- Make physics modeling decisions without flagging them to the user
- Suppress or bypass test failures

#!/bin/bash
# Test the hit format pre-commit hook behavior.
# Creates an isolated git repo, installs the hook logic directly, generates dummy input files
# (well formatted and poorly formatted) and verifies
# it accepts well-formatted .i files and rejects poorly-formatted ones.

REPO_DIR="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"
HIT="$REPO_DIR/moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit"

pass=0
fail=0

run_test() {
    local desc="$1"
    local expected="$2"  # "pass" or "fail"
    local actual_exit="$3"

    if [[ "$expected" == "pass" && "$actual_exit" == "0" ]] || \
       [[ "$expected" == "fail" && "$actual_exit" != "0" ]]; then
        echo "PASS: $desc"
        ((pass++))
    else
        echo "FAIL: $desc (expected commit to $expected, got exit code $actual_exit)"
        ((fail++))
    fi
}

if [[ ! -x "$HIT" ]]; then
    echo "SKIP: hit binary not found at $HIT (build MOOSE first)"
    exit 0
fi

# Set up isolated temp git repo
tmpdir=$(mktemp -d)
trap "rm -rf '$tmpdir'" EXIT

cd "$tmpdir"
git init -q
git config user.email "test@test.com"
git config user.name "Test"

# Point moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit at the real binary via symlink
mkdir -p moose/framework/contrib/hit
ln -s "$HIT" moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit

# Install the hit-format portion of the pre-commit hook directly
mkdir -p .git/hooks
cat > .git/hooks/pre-commit << 'HOOKEOF'
#!/bin/bash
REPO_DIR="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"
HIT="$REPO_DIR/moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit"

if [[ ! -x "$HIT" ]]; then
    echo "Warning: hit binary not found at $HIT, skipping .i file format check" >&2
else
    staged_i_files=$(git diff --staged --name-only -- '*.i')
    if [[ -n "$staged_i_files" ]]; then
        needs_format=false
        while IFS= read -r f; do
            [[ -f "$f" ]] || continue
            tmpfile=$(mktemp)
            git show ":$f" > "$tmpfile"
            "$HIT" format "$tmpfile" 2>/dev/null
            staged_content=$(git show ":$f")
            formatted_content=$(cat "$tmpfile")
            rm -f "$tmpfile"
            if [[ "$staged_content" != "$formatted_content" ]]; then
                echo "File '$f' needs hit formatting. Run: $HIT format $f" >&2
                needs_format=true
            fi
        done <<< "$staged_i_files"
        if $needs_format; then
            echo "" >&2
            echo "Run the above command(s) and re-stage the affected files." >&2
            exit 1
        fi
    fi
fi
HOOKEOF
chmod +x .git/hooks/pre-commit

# Initial empty commit so git diff --staged has a base to compare against
git commit -q --allow-empty -m "init"

# --- Test 1: No .i files staged — hook should pass ---
touch dummy.txt
git add dummy.txt
git commit -q -m "no i files" 2>/dev/null
run_test "No .i files staged — hook passes" "pass" $?

# --- Test 2: Properly formatted .i file — hook should pass ---
cat > good.i << 'EOF'
[Mesh]
  type = GeneratedMesh
  dim = 1
[]
EOF
"$HIT" format good.i  # canonicalize before staging
git add good.i
git commit -q -m "formatted i file" 2>/dev/null
run_test "Properly formatted .i file — hook passes" "pass" $?

# --- Test 3: Poorly formatted .i file — hook should reject ---
# Missing spaces around '=' and missing indentation are formatting violations
printf '[Mesh]\ntype=GeneratedMesh\ndim=1\n[]\n' > bad.i
git add bad.i
git commit -q -m "unformatted i file" 2>/dev/null
run_test "Poorly formatted .i file — hook rejects" "fail" $?

# --- Test 4: Fixed file after rejection — hook should pass ---
"$HIT" format bad.i
git add bad.i
git commit -q -m "fixed i file" 2>/dev/null
run_test "Re-formatted .i file after rejection — hook passes" "pass" $?

echo ""
echo "Results: $pass passed, $fail failed"
[[ $fail -eq 0 ]]

# AGENTS.md

## Environment
- Use the MOOSE conda environment (`conda activate moose`)
- If `./tmap8-opt` or `run_tests` throws an error, stop and ask the user
- Pre-commit hooks handle all formatting and input file validation on every `git commit` — do not invoke `clang-format`, `black`, or `hit format` manually
- `moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit check <file>.i` is available to validate input files during development — use it before committing

## Before writing any code or input file
- Browse `doc/content/` to understand documentation standards and existing patterns before starting work
- Before writing a new C++ class, look through existing MOOSE classes in the codebase to confirm expected syntax and patterns
- Documentation in `doc/content/` may not accurately reflect current code behavior — once you identify a class to use in an input file, find its C++ implementation and read it to confirm the class does what you expect before writing the input file

## C++ code
- Every new class requires a MOOSE test system test in `test/tests/` before committing
- Every new class requires a MooseDocs page following existing TMAP8 doc structure
- Never hardcode physical parameters — first check if it is already defined in `doc/content/source/utils/TMAP8PhysicalConstants`, and if not available, expose everything through `addParam`
- Code is unit-agnostic; units live in input files only

## Input files
- Before writing an input file, look through existing MOOSE and TMAP8 classes to confirm object names and parameter syntax
- Use `moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit check <file>.i` to validate before committing
- Use `./tmap8-opt -i <file>.i` to run input files
- Write parameter values in the units the reference source used; convert explicitly using the MOOSE units system with the conversion documented in a comment
- State the unit system at the top of every input file in a comment block

## Testing
- Run tests only when `.C`, `.h`, or `test/tests/` files change
- Run only the affected module: `./run_tests -j<N> --re <module>`

## Session log
- Every session must be associated with a GitHub issue on `idaholab/TMAP8`; ask the user for the issue number at the start of the session if one is not provided, or create one if the user instructs you to
- **SQA requirement:** When creating a GitHub issue with `gh`, you must use the appropriate template from `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`; read the available templates first, select the one that fits (e.g. `bug-report.md` for bugs, `feature-request.md` for new features or tasks), and populate all sections in the template body — passing a bare description without following the template structure is not acceptable
- Before committing, copy the raw `.jsonl` session file for the current session into `doc/content/agent/<issue-number>/`; the session file is the most recently modified `.jsonl` under `~/.claude/projects/-Users-bhavcv-projects-TMAP8/`

## Committing
- Commit at minimum after: every input file that successfully runs, every test that passes or fails, and every new class added
- If a test or run failed, commit the failure state first (include the full error message in the commit body), then commit the fix separately
- Every commit must reference the associated GitHub issue (e.g. `Refs #<issue-number>` or `Closes #<issue-number>` as appropriate) in the commit message
- **Every commit must include an updated copy of the session `.jsonl`** — before staging, copy the current session file from `~/.claude/projects/-Users-bhavcv-projects-TMAP8/<session-id>.jsonl` to `doc/content/agent/<issue-number>/` and stage it; this is a critical SQA requirement
- Every commit must include these trailers:
```
Refs #<issue-number>
AI-assisted: true
AI-model: <model name>
AI-version: <model version>
Co-Authored-By: <model name> <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

## Pull requests
- PR description must include: physics/modeling assumptions made, AI reasoning log (decision points and alternatives considered), and tests added
- Never merge autonomously

## Never do
- Modify existing passing tests without explicit user instruction
- Modify the build system without explicit user instruction
- Make physics modeling decisions without flagging them to the user
- Suppress or bypass test failures

#!/bin/bash
# Test the hit format pre-commit hook behavior.
# Creates an isolated git repo, installs the hook logic directly, generates dummy input files
# (well formatted and poorly formatted) and verifies
# it accepts well-formatted .i files and rejects poorly-formatted ones.

REPO_DIR="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"
HIT="$REPO_DIR/moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit"

pass=0
fail=0

run_test() {
    local desc="$1"
    local expected="$2"  # "pass" or "fail"
    local actual_exit="$3"

    if [[ "$expected" == "pass" && "$actual_exit" == "0" ]] || \
       [[ "$expected" == "fail" && "$actual_exit" != "0" ]]; then
        echo "PASS: $desc"
        ((pass++))
    else
        echo "FAIL: $desc (expected commit to $expected, got exit code $actual_exit)"
        ((fail++))
    fi
}

if [[ ! -x "$HIT" ]]; then
    echo "SKIP: hit binary not found at $HIT (build MOOSE first)"
    exit 0
fi

# Set up isolated temp git repo
tmpdir=$(mktemp -d)
trap "rm -rf '$tmpdir'" EXIT

cd "$tmpdir"
git init -q
git config user.email "test@test.com"
git config user.name "Test"

# Point moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit at the real binary via symlink
mkdir -p moose/framework/contrib/hit
ln -s "$HIT" moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit

# Install the hit-format portion of the pre-commit hook directly
mkdir -p .git/hooks
cat > .git/hooks/pre-commit << 'HOOKEOF'
#!/bin/bash
REPO_DIR="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"
HIT="$REPO_DIR/moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit"

if [[ ! -x "$HIT" ]]; then
    echo "Warning: hit binary not found at $HIT, skipping .i file format check" >&2
else
    staged_i_files=$(git diff --staged --name-only -- '*.i')
    if [[ -n "$staged_i_files" ]]; then
        needs_format=false
        while IFS= read -r f; do
            [[ -f "$f" ]] || continue
            tmpfile=$(mktemp)
            git show ":$f" > "$tmpfile"
            "$HIT" format "$tmpfile" 2>/dev/null
            staged_content=$(git show ":$f")
            formatted_content=$(cat "$tmpfile")
            rm -f "$tmpfile"
            if [[ "$staged_content" != "$formatted_content" ]]; then
                echo "File '$f' needs hit formatting. Run: $HIT format $f" >&2
                needs_format=true
            fi
        done <<< "$staged_i_files"
        if $needs_format; then
            echo "" >&2
            echo "Run the above command(s) and re-stage the affected files." >&2
            exit 1
        fi
    fi
fi
HOOKEOF
chmod +x .git/hooks/pre-commit

# Initial empty commit so git diff --staged has a base to compare against
git commit -q --allow-empty -m "init"

# --- Test 1: No .i files staged — hook should pass ---
touch dummy.txt
git add dummy.txt
git commit -q -m "no i files" 2>/dev/null
run_test "No .i files staged — hook passes" "pass" $?

# --- Test 2: Properly formatted .i file — hook should pass ---
cat > good.i << 'EOF'
[Mesh]
  type = GeneratedMesh
  dim = 1
[]
EOF
"$HIT" format good.i  # canonicalize before staging
git add good.i
git commit -q -m "formatted i file" 2>/dev/null
run_test "Properly formatted .i file — hook passes" "pass" $?

# --- Test 3: Poorly formatted .i file — hook should reject ---
# Missing spaces around '=' and missing indentation are formatting violations
printf '[Mesh]\ntype=GeneratedMesh\ndim=1\n[]\n' > bad.i
git add bad.i
git commit -q -m "unformatted i file" 2>/dev/null
run_test "Poorly formatted .i file — hook rejects" "fail" $?

# --- Test 4: Fixed file after rejection — hook should pass ---
"$HIT" format bad.i
git add bad.i
git commit -q -m "fixed i file" 2>/dev/null
run_test "Re-formatted .i file after rejection — hook passes" "pass" $?

echo ""
echo "Results: $pass passed, $fail failed"
[[ $fail -eq 0 ]]

input.old_string:
if [[ ! -x "$HIT" ]]; then
    echo "SKIP: hit binary not found at $HIT (build MOOSE first)"
    exit 0
fi
input.new_string:
if [[ ! -x "$HIT" ]] || ! "$HIT" --help >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "SKIP: hit binary not found or not functional at $HIT (build MOOSE first or check MPI libraries)"
    exit 0
fi

message.content: Question. Why did we end up with a new jsonl file for the same session?

content.text:
Each Claude Code conversation gets a unique session ID (UUID), so a new `.jsonl` file is created for every new conversation. The previous session that worked on issue #396 had its own UUID and jsonl; this conversation is a separate session with its own UUID (`09cdaa97...`).

That's actually exactly the scenario the updated AGENTS.md rule covers — "if multiple Claude/Codex sessions contribute to the same branch and issue, all session files must be kept." So having two jsonl files in `doc/content/agent/396/` is correct behavior: one per contributing session.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/bhavcv/.claude/skills/moose-docs

# MOOSE Markdown Documentation Skill

This skill helps write MOOSE documentation using MooseDocs markdown extensions.

## File Organization

- Framework docs: `framework/doc/content/source/[type]/ClassName.md`
- Module docs: `modules/[module]/doc/content/source/[type]/ClassName.md`
- Each `.md` file corresponds to a `.C` file containing `registerMooseObject()`
- File name must exactly match the class name (case-sensitive)

## Standard Object Documentation Structure

```markdown
# ClassName

!syntax description /ObjectType/ClassName

## Description

`ClassName` [description of what the object does, mentioning the class name
without spaces in the first paragraph].

[Detailed explanation with equations if relevant]

## Example Input Syntax

!listing path/to/test/file.i block=BlockName

!syntax parameters /ObjectType/ClassName

!syntax inputs /ObjectType/ClassName

!syntax children /ObjectType/ClassName
```

For classes with AD variants, use: `# ClassName / ADClassName`

The `!syntax description` line is optional if you're writing a full description yourself.

## Core Syntax Commands

### Automatic Content Generation

```markdown
!syntax description /Kernels/Diffusion     # Class description from addClassDescription()
!syntax parameters /Kernels/Diffusion      # Parameter table from validParams()
!syntax inputs /Kernels/Diffusion          # List of input files using this object
!syntax children /Kernels/Diffusion        # Child classes that inherit from this
!syntax list /Kernels                      # List all objects in a system
```

The syntax path matches the input file structure: `/SystemType/ClassName` (e.g., `/Kernels/Diffusion`, `/BCs/DirichletBC`, `/Materials/GenericConstantMaterial`)

## Parameter References

Use `[!param]()` to link to parameter documentation:

```markdown
The [!param](/Kernels/Diffusion/variable) parameter specifies the variable.
Set [!param](/Materials/DerivativeParsedMaterial/coupled_variables) to list coupled variables.
```

The path format is: `/SystemType/ClassName/parameter_name`

This corresponds to the input file syntax structure:
- `[Kernels]` block -> `/Kernels/`
- Object type (e.g., `type = Diffusion`) -> `/Kernels/Diffusion/`
- Parameter name (e.g., `variable = u`) -> `/Kernels/Diffusion/variable`

## Linking Conventions

**Page links do NOT use leading slashes.** The system searches for matching filenames:

```markdown
[Diffusion](Diffusion.md)                    # Links to Diffusion.md page
[core.md]                                    # Shortcut - uses first heading as text
[text](core.md#heading-id)                   # With bookmark to specific heading
[syntax/Kernels/index.md]                    # Partial path for disambiguation
```

**Source code links DO use leading slashes:**
```markdown
[/Diffusion.C]                               # Opens modal with full source
[Diffusion Kernel](/Diffusion.C)             # Custom link text
[`run_tests`](/test/run_tests language=python)  # With syntax highlighting
```

The distinction:
- `.md` files (documentation pages): NO leading slash
- `.C`, `.h`, `.py` files (source code): Leading slash for modal display

## Code Listings

Include code from repository files (never copy-paste):

```markdown
!listing framework/src/kernels/Diffusion.C                    # Full file
!listing test/tests/kernels/simple_diffusion/simple_diffusion.i block=Kernels  # HIT block
!listing framework/src/kernels/Diffusion.C start=computeQp end=}               # Line range
!listing path/to/file.i block=Kernels id=example caption=Example usage.        # With caption
```

## Mathematical Equations

Inline: `$\nabla \cdot \nabla u = 0$`

Display with label:
```markdown
\begin{equation}
R_i(u_h) = (\nabla \psi_i, \nabla u_h) = 0 \quad \forall \psi_i
\label{eq:weak-form}
\end{equation}
```

Formatting standards:
- Powers: ` \times 10^{-6}$` (not `\cdot`)
- Exponentials: `$\exp \left( x \right)$` (not `e^x`)
- Units: `$\mathrm{\mu}$m` (upright font)
- Always blank line between equation and explanation

## Alerts

```markdown
!alert note
This is a note.

!alert warning title=Important
This is a warning with custom title.

!alert tip title=Pro Tip
Helpful tip here.

!alert error
Error message.
```

Block version for complex content:
```markdown
!alert! warning title=Caution
- Item one
- Item two
!alert-end!
```

## Images and Media

```markdown
!media path/to/image.png
       style=width:50%;
       id=fig-example
       caption=Description of the figure.
```

Always include `caption=` and `id=` for figures.

## Citations

```markdown
[!cite](reference_key)           # Smith et al. (2020)
[!citep](reference_key)          # (Smith et al., 2020)
[!cite](ref1, ref2)              # Multiple citations
```

Add references to appropriate `.bib` files in `doc/` directories.

## Tables

```markdown
!table id=my-table caption=Table description.
| Column 1 | Column 2 |
| :- | -: |
| Left aligned | Right aligned |
```

## Writing Guidelines

1. **Target end-users** - Focus on usage, not implementation
2. **Include theory** - Explain the physics/math with equations
3. **Show working examples** - Use `!listing` to reference test files
4. **Document limitations** - Note validity ranges and assumptions
5. **Use consistent headings** - Only `##` for main sections (appears in sidebar)
6. **Class name in first paragraph** - Mention the class name without spaces
7. **No implementation details** - Avoid inheritance info, internal workings

## Cross-Referencing Other Documentation

Link to related objects without leading slashes:

```markdown
## Similar Objects

- For a function-based value: [FunctionDirichletBC.md]
- Using a penalty method: [PenaltyDirichletBC.md]
- See the [Kernels overview](syntax/Kernels/index.md)
```

## Complete Example

A boundary condition documentation page:

```markdown
# DirichletBC

!syntax description /BCs/DirichletBC

## Description

`DirichletBC` is the simplest type of `NodalBC`, and is used for
imposing so-called "essential" boundary conditions on systems of
partial differential equations (PDEs). Such boundary conditions force
a particular set of degrees of freedom (DOFs) defined by the
`boundary` parameter to take on a single, controllable value.

This class is appropriate to use for PDEs of the form

\begin{equation}
\begin{aligned}
  -\nabla^2 u &= f && \quad \in \Omega \\
  u &= g && \quad \in \partial \Omega_D
\end{aligned}
\end{equation}

where $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is the domain.

## Similar Dirichlet BCs

- To use a Function instead of a constant value: [FunctionDirichletBC.md]
- To impose a Dirichlet BC using a penalty method: [PenaltyDirichletBC.md]

## Example Input Syntax

!listing test/tests/bcs/dirichlet_bc/dirichlet_bc_test.i block=BCs

The [!param](/BCs/DirichletBC/value) parameter sets the boundary value.

!syntax parameters /BCs/DirichletBC

!syntax inputs /BCs/DirichletBC

!syntax children /BCs/DirichletBC
```

## Finding the Correct Syntax Path

The syntax path corresponds to input file structure:
- Look at how the object is used in input files
- A kernel used as `[Kernels] [diff] type = Diffusion` has path `/Kernels/Diffusion`
- A BC used as `[BCs] [left] type = DirichletBC` has path `/BCs/DirichletBC`
- Parameters are appended: `/BCs/DirichletBC/value`

Check `registerMooseObject()` calls in `.C` files to find the registered name.

## Finding Test Input Files for Examples

Use grep to find test files that use a particular object:

```bash
grep -r "type = ClassName" test/tests/ modules/*/test/tests/
```

Good example files:
- Should demonstrate typical usage
- Should be simple and focused
- Avoid files that test edge cases or error conditions

## Generating Documentation Stubs

For new objects, generate a stub file:

```bash
cd modules/[module]/doc
./moosedocs.py generate [AppName]
```

This creates template files that you can then fill in with content.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Leading slashes on page links** - Use `[FunctionDirichletBC.md]` not `[/FunctionDirichletBC.md]`
2. **Missing class name in first paragraph** - Always mention `ClassName` in the description
3. **Copy-pasting code** - Use `!listing` to include code from repository
4. **Implementation details** - Focus on usage, not internal workings
5. **Missing blank line after equations** - Required before "where..." explanations
6. **Wrong heading levels** - Only `##` appears in sidebar navigation

## Test Specification Requirements

When adding new tests, include documentation metadata in the `tests` file:

```
[Tests]
  [my_test]
    type = 'Exodiff'
    input = 'my_test.i'
    exodiff = 'my_test_out.e'

    requirement = "The system shall compute the diffusion equation correctly."
    design = 'Diffusion.md'
    issues = '#1234'
  []
[]
```

- `requirement`: Describes what the test verifies (complete sentence)
- `design`: Links to relevant documentation files (partial paths OK)
- `issues`: GitHub issue numbers associated with this feature/fix

1→# Contributing to TMAP8
     2→
     3→TMAP8 is a collaborative software project, and all contributions to the code are welcome! Because
     4→multiple developers and groups are working on and using TMAP8, we have various standards, procedures,
     5→and testing processes to maintain the software quality of this project. Below, contribution guidelines
     6→and development best practices for TMAP8 are outlined.
     7→
     8→## Follow the TMAP8 code standards
     9→
    10→!style halign=left
    11→When modifying TMAP8, the development team requests that all [Code Standards](tmap8_scs.md) be
    12→followed. Additionally, if you are creating a new verification or validation case, make sure to follow the [V&V standards](/verification_and_validation_standards.md). These guidelines make sure that the repository code follows a consistent look and feel to
    13→make development easier.
    14→
    15→## Creating and Referencing Issues
    16→
    17→!style halign=left
    18→When finding an issue with the code, or developing/suggesting a new feature or enhancement, an issue
    19→should be created. This can be done ahead of time when developing code, or anytime before pushing
    20→your changes. In any case, you will need to list an issue number in one of your commits! To get started:
    21→
    22→1. Select “New issue” on this page: [https://github.com/idaholab/TMAP8/issues](https://github.com/idaholab/TMAP8/issues)
    23→1. Select either “bug report” or “feature request” (most should be feature requests)
    24→1. Provide issue descriptions of a couple of sentences (or as much as you see fit) following the prompts.
    25→1. Create issue
    26→
    27→You will now have an issue number next to the title. We will use it to link the pull request (PR) to the issue.
    28→
    29→## Work in a GitHub fork
    30→
    31→!style halign=left
    32→TMAP8 development follows a "Fork & Pull" development process. See more information on
    33→[forking repositories in the GitHub documentation](https://guides.github.com/activities/forking).
    34→
    35→### Fork TMAP8 and clone
    36→
    37→1. Navigate to [the TMAP8 repository](https://github.com/idaholab/TMAP8)
    38→1. Click the "Fork" button on the upper right of the code file tree to have a copy of TMAP8 in your own account
    39→1. Clone your new fork to your local machine using the following command (this assumes you
    40→   [have SSH set up on your GitHub account](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/adding-a-new-ssh-key-to-your-github-account)):
    41→
    42→   ```
    43→   cd ~/projects
    44→   git clone git@github.com:<your_user_name>/tmap8.git
    45→   ```
    46→
    47→   In your local copy, this clone URL is given the shorthand name "origin". This will become relevant
    48→   later in this document.
    49→
    50→### Add an upstream git remote
    51→
    52→!style halign=left
    53→Add the main TMAP8 repository as an "upstream" git remote, so that you can
    54→[git fetch](https://git-scm.org/docs/git-fetch) updates from the main repository:
    55→
    56→```
    57→cd ~/projects/TMAP8
    58→git remote add upstream git@github.com:idaholab/TMAP8.git
    59→```
    60→
    61→To fetch changes, run:
    62→
    63→```
    64→git fetch upstream
    65→```
    66→
    67→## Creating a new branch
    68→
    69→Before we make any changes locally (which would require having a working version of the application),
    70→we will need to create a new branch.
    71→
    72→1. In your terminal, go to your application:
    73→
    74→   ```
    75→   cd ~/projects/tmap8
    76→   ```
    77→
    78→1. It is usually best to make sure your changes are based on devel, so check it out for use:
    79→
    80→   ```
    81→   git checkout devel
    82→   ```
    83→
    84→1. Fetch and reset devel to get it up to date (make sure you do not have any changes on devel, because you would lose that):
    85→
    86→   ```
    87→   git fetch upstream && git reset --hard upstream/devel && git submodule update --init
    88→   ```
    89→
    90→1. Create a new branch, selecting an appropriate branch name for your development activities:
    91→
    92→   ```
    93→   git checkout -b <BRANCH_NAME>
    94→   ```
    95→
    96→## Make modifications and commit (the regular development workflow)
    97→
    98→At this point, you have a local branch that is up to date with the upstream remote. You’re now ready
    99→to make your changes. Remember, **small, consistent changes** are much better than dumping massive,
   100→unrelated changes all at once.
   101→
   102→1. Make your changes locally to address the issue (partly or fully) - using
   103→   [VSCode is recommended](help/development/VSCode.md).
   104→1. In your terminal, go to your application:
   105→
   106→   ```
   107→   cd ~/projects/tmap8
   108→   ```
   109→
   110→1. Make sure that the app still compiles and that the tests run:
   111→
   112→   ```
   113→   make -j6 && ./run_tests -j6
   114→   ```
   115→
   116→   If you modified the documentation, you can then build and test it locally:
   117→
   118→   ```
   119→   cd ~/projects/tmap8/doc && ./moosedocs.py build --serve
   120→   ```
   121→
   122→1. Type `git status` to see the status of your branch. That should show you the changed files and
   123→   give you some commands to stage them.
   124→1. Once you are ready, perform
   125→
   126→   ```
   127→   git add <FILE_NAME_1> <FILE_NAME_2> <FILE_NAME_3> ...
   128→   ```
   129→
   130→   to stage your updated files.
   131→1. At that point, it is usually good practice to run
   132→
   133→   ```
   134→   git clang-format HEAD~1
   135→   ```
   136→
   137→   to fix the formatting of the files based on the `.clang-format` file in the root of the repository.
   138→   If files are changed, run
   139→
   140→   ```
   141→   git add <...>
   142→   ```
   143→
   144→   for these files. Using `git status` again to check for changed files is helpful here.
   145→1. Now you’re ready to commit. Run
   146→
   147→   ```
   148→   git commit
   149→   ```
   150→
   151→1. A file editor will appear. Now enter a short description of the commit.
   152→   If you use VIM / VI as the default terminal editor, press the `i` key, and you’ll be able to edit the file.
   153→   It is good practice to have a short title, and then a list of items describing the
   154→   changes in more detail. At the bottom, you should add `(Ref. #<ISSUE_NUMBER>)`. It will look
   155→   something like this:
   156→
   157→   ```
   158→   Updating this to enable that:
   159→   - Update this file to do this
   160→   - Update documentation to reflect that change
   161→   - Create new test for new capability
   162→   - Update existing gold files to reflect change
   163→
   164→   (Ref. #1234)
   165→   ```
   166→
   167→1. To exit and save, press `esc`, then type `:wq` if you use VIM / VI as the editor. The commit file will close.
   168→1. You might have to do several commits to capture all your changes.
   169→
   170→## Generating new documentation pages using MooseDocs
   171→
   172→During the course of development, especially during the creation of new TMAP8 objects (e.g., kernels,
   173→boundary conditions, interface conditions, etc.), it is important to create documentation outlining
   174→any new capabilities. Templates for object documents can be generated using the [MooseDocs system](MooseDocs/index.md)
   175→using the "generate" sub-command. To generate templates for new objects, run:
   176→
   177→```
   178→cd ~/projects/tmap8
   179→./moosedocs.py generate app_types TMAP8App
   180→```
   181→
   182→For example, with a new object called `TMAP8Diffusion` without documentation, the following output will be seen:
   183→
   184→```
   185→% ./moosedocs.py generate app_types TMAP8App
   186→Creating/updating stub page: /Users/username/projects/tmap8/doc/content/source/kernels/TMAP8Diffusion.md
   187→CRITICAL:0 ERROR:0 WARNING:0
   188→```
   189→
   190→And the following template would be created:
   191→
   192→```markdown
   193→# TMAP8Diffusion
   194→
   195→!alert construction title=Undocumented Class
   196→The TMAP8Diffusion has not been documented. The content listed below should be used as a starting point for
   197→documenting the class, which includes the typical automatic documentation associated with a
   198→MooseObject; however, what is contained is ultimately determined by what is necessary to make the
   199→documentation clear for users.
   200→
   201→!syntax description /Kernels/TMAP8Diffusion
   202→
   203→## Overview
   204→
   205→!! Replace these lines with information regarding the TMAP8Diffusion object.
   206→
   207→## Example Input File Syntax
   208→
   209→!! Describe and include an example of how to use the TMAP8Diffusion object.
   210→
   211→!syntax parameters /Kernels/TMAP8Diffusion
   212→
   213→!syntax inputs /Kernels/TMAP8Diffusion
   214→
   215→!syntax children /Kernels/TMAP8Diffusion
   216→```
   217→
   218→Note that certain items, such as the source code description, object parameters, inputs in which the
   219→object is used, and child objects are filled in automatically using the [MooseDocs/extensions/appsyntax.md].
   220→The lines leading with `!!` as well as the `!alert` extension command should be removed and replaced
   221→with relevant documentation regarding how to use the object.
   222→
   223→!alert! note title=Use good documentation practices!
   224→When creating documentation, it is particularly important to note any design limitations or assumptions
   225→as well as best practices to apply when using the object.
   226→!alert-end!
   227→
   228→Again, make sure to commit these changes using the instructions above before pushing your branch.
   229→
   230→## Pushing and submitting TMAP8 changes
   231→
   232→At some point, you'll be ready to push your work to your fork and submit the changes as a pull request
   233→(PR). To do this follow the following steps.
   234→
   235→1. To push your work to your fork (remember, it has the remote name `origin`) run:
   236→
   237→   ```
   238→   git push origin <BRANCH_NAME>
   239→   ```
   240→
   241→1. A link will be printed in the terminal; visit that page.
   242→1. There, you’ll find instructions to create your PR. Fill out the form (one can simply copy what is
   243→   in the commit messages, or write a custom summary of your own) and submit your PR!
   244→1. To notify others (or the TMAP8 development team), you can also tag people in your PR to get their
   245→   attention.
   246→

1→# AGENTS.md
     2→
     3→## Environment
     4→- Use the MOOSE conda environment (`conda activate moose`)
     5→- If `./tmap8-opt` or `run_tests` throws an error, stop and ask the user
     6→- Pre-commit hooks handle all formatting and input file validation on every `git commit` — do not invoke `clang-format`, `black`, or `hit format` manually
     7→- `moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit check <file>.i` is available to validate input files during development — use it before committing
     8→
     9→## Before writing any code or input file
    10→- Browse `doc/content/` to understand documentation standards and existing patterns before starting work
    11→- Before writing a new C++ class, look through existing MOOSE classes in the codebase to confirm expected syntax and patterns
    12→- Documentation in `doc/content/` may not accurately reflect current code behavior — once you identify a class to use in an input file, find its C++ implementation and read it to confirm the class does what you expect before writing the input file
    13→
    14→## C++ code
    15→- Every new class requires a MOOSE TestHarness test in `test/tests/` before committing
    16→- Every new class requires a MooseDocs page following existing TMAP8 doc structure
    17→- Never hardcode physical parameters — first check if it is already defined in `doc/content/source/utils/TMAP8PhysicalConstants`, and if not available, expose everything through `addParam`
    18→- Code is unit-agnostic; units live in input files only
    19→
    20→## Input files
    21→- Before writing an input file, look through existing MOOSE and TMAP8 classes to confirm object names and parameter syntax
    22→- Use `moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit check <file>.i` to validate before committing
    23→- Use `./tmap8-opt -i <file>.i` to run input files
    24→- Write parameter values in the units the reference source used; use the MOOSE units system for conversion — the units system is self-documenting and does not require inline comments
    25→- State the unit system at the top of every input file in a comment block
    26→
    27→## Testing
    28→- Run tests only when `.C`, `.h`, or `test/tests/` files change
    29→- Run only the affected module: `./run_tests -j<N> --re <module>`
    30→
    31→## Session log
    32→- Every session must be associated with a GitHub issue on `idaholab/TMAP8`; ask the user for the issue number at the start of the session if one is not provided, or create one if the user instructs you to
    33→- **SQA requirement:** When creating a GitHub issue with `gh`, you must use the appropriate template from `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`; read the available templates first, select the one that fits (e.g. `bug-report.md` for bugs, `feature-request.md` for new features or tasks), and populate all sections in the template body — passing a bare description without following the template structure is not acceptable
    34→- Before committing, copy the current session's `.jsonl` file into `doc/content/agent/<issue-number>/`. For Claude Code sessions the file is the most recently modified `.jsonl` under `~/.claude/projects/<repo-slug>/`; for Codex sessions check the equivalent Codex session directory. Do not remove old session files already in that directory — append the new one alongside them.
    35→
    36→## Committing
    37→- Commit at minimum after: every input file that successfully runs, every test that passes or fails, and every new class added
    38→- If a test or run failed, commit the failure state first (include the full error message in the commit body), then commit the fix separately
    39→- Every commit must reference the associated GitHub issue (e.g. `Refs #<issue-number>` or `Closes #<issue-number>` as appropriate) in the commit message
    40→- **Every commit must include an updated copy of the session `.jsonl`** — before staging, copy the current session file from `~/.claude/projects/<repo-slug>/<session-id>.jsonl` to `doc/content/agent/<issue-number>/` and stage it; this is a critical SQA requirement. Do not remove session files already present in that directory — if multiple Claude/Codex sessions contribute to the same branch and issue, all session files must be kept.
    41→- Every commit must include these trailers:
    42→```
    43→Refs #<issue-number>
    44→AI-assisted: true
    45→AI-model: <model name>
    46→AI-version: <model version>
    47→Co-Authored-By: <model name> <noreply@anthropic.com>
    48→```
    49→
    50→## Pull requests
    51→- PR description must include: physics/modeling assumptions made, AI reasoning log (decision points and alternatives considered), and tests added
    52→- Never merge autonomously
    53→
    54→## Never do
    55→- Modify existing passing tests without explicit user instruction
    56→- Modify the build system without explicit user instruction
    57→- Make physics modeling decisions without flagging them to the user
    58→- Suppress or bypass test failures
    59→

1→# The `hit` command
     2→
     3→*Hierarchical Input Text* (HIT) is the file format that MOOSE input files (and
     4→`tests` specs) are built upon. HIT is a key-value pair syntax with multilevel
     5→block hierarchies. To deal with HIT files we provide the `hit` executable, which
     6→is automatically built under `framework/contrib/hit`. This command line tool
     7→offers multiple functions for
     8→
     9→- Finding parameters across many input files in a context sensitive way
    10→- Validating input files
    11→- Formatting input files according to a user defined style
    12→- Merging input files
    13→- Diffing input files
    14→- Extracting common portions of input files
    15→- Subtracting out common portions of input files
    16→
    17→## `hit find` - Searching for parameters
    18→
    19→```
    20→hit find [-i] [-v] [-p additional_pattern [...] --] main_pattern files ...
    21→```
    22→
    23→`hit find` looks for a full parameter path that can be specified with wildcards
    24→as `main_pattern`. `*` will match an arbitrary set of characters and `?` will
    25→match a single character. In the input
    26→
    27→```
    28→[Kernels]
    29→  [diff]
    30→    type = Diffusion
    31→    variable = a
    32→  []
    33→[]
    34→```
    35→
    36→the main pattern `Kernels/*/variable` will match the variable line, as will
    37→`Kernels/*/variable=a`, `*iable=?`, and `*/diff/*=a`, but `Kernels/*/variable=b`
    38→will not match.
    39→
    40→An `!=` operator is also available for negative matching. In the example above
    41→`Kernels/*/variable!=b` will match the variable line.
    42→
    43→The `-i` option will turn all matching case insensitive (useful to match
    44→MooseEnum values).
    45→
    46→The `-v` option will list all files that do not have a single match, which can
    47→be used to check for the absence of patterns.
    48→
    49→The `-p` option will match additional parameters inside the blocks matched by
    50→the main pattern. For example `hit find -p type=Diffusion -- Kernels/*/variable=a`
    51→will match the snippet above, and any kernel acting on the variable `a` that has
    52→`type = Diffusion` (and so would `hit find -p type!=NeumannBC -- Kernels/*/variable=a`)
    53→
    54→### Examples
    55→
    56→```
    57→hit find Outputs/file_base **/*.i
    58→```
    59→
    60→Will find all inputs that contain the `file_base` parameter in the `[Outputs]` block.
    61→
    62→```
    63→hit find Outputs/file_base **/*.i
    64→```
    65→
    66→```
    67→hit find -v Physics/SolidMechanics/QuasiStatic/* **/*.i
    68→```
    69→
    70→Will find all files that do not (`-v`) contain use of the SolidMechanics QuasiStatic Physics
    71→action.
    72→
    73→```
    74→hit find BCs/*/boundary=left **/*.i
    75→```
    76→
    77→Will find all boundary conditions that are applied to just the `left` sideset.
    78→
    79→```
    80→hit find BCs/*/boundary!=left **/*.i
    81→```
    82→
    83→Will find all boundary conditions that are *not* applied to just the `left` sideset.
    84→
    85→```
    86→hit find -p type=FunctionDirichletBC -- BCs/*/boundary=\*right\* **/*.i
    87→```
    88→
    89→Will find all `FunctionDirichletBC`s that are applied to the `right` sideset
    90→(and possibly others). This will also match `boundary=inner_right` so some
    91→filtering of the results with `grep` may be necessary.
    92→
    93→```
    94→hit find -i -p formulation=mortar mortar_approach -- Contact/*/model=coulomb **/*.i
    95→```
    96→
    97→Will find all inputs that contain a `Contact` action block with `model = COULOMB`
    98→(case insensitive match using `-i`) and have the `formulation` parameter set to
    99→`mortar` *and* have also specified a `mortar_approach` parameter (with whatever
   100→value). Not that multiple arguments are supplied to the `-p` parameter and the
   101→list is terminated with a double dash `--`, after which the main pattern and the
   102→list of files to search follow.
   103→
   104→## `hit format` - Formatting input files
   105→
   106→```
   107→hit format [-i] [-style file] input
   108→```
   109→
   110→The format subcommand will reformat a valid HIT file into a canonical form with
   111→a consistent indentation and potentially sorted sections and parameters. The
   112→name of the file to be formatted is given as the `input` parameter. When
   113→specifying `-` as the filename the input is taken from stdin.
   114→
   115→The formatted input will be output to terminal unless the `-i` option is supplied,
   116→which enables inplace formatting of the supplied `input` file.
   117→
   118→The formatting style can be user defined using the `-style` parameter. The
   119→argument is the name of a style file of the format:
   120→
   121→```
   122→    [format]
   123→        indent_string = "  "
   124→        line_length = 100
   125→        canonical_section_markers = true
   126→
   127→        [sorting]
   128→            [pattern]
   129→                section = "[^/]+/[^/]+"
   130→                order = "type"
   131→            []
   132→            [pattern]
   133→                section = ""
   134→                order = "Mesh ** Executioner Outputs"
   135→            []
   136→        []
   137→    []
   138→```
   139→
   140→where all fields are optional and the sorting section is also optional.  If the
   141→sorting section is present, you can have as many patterns as you want, but each
   142→pattern section must have 'section' and 'order' fields.
   143→
   144→Note that single `'` and double `"` quotes behave differently when auto-formatting input files with `hit format`.
   145→Single quotes strings will not be reformatted, while double quoted strings are reindented
   146→and reflowed. *Multiline strings* will only be reindented through inserting or removing spaces on the lines
   147→following the first line, to match any changes in indentation of the first line. *Single line strings*
   148→will be reflowed according to the set `line_length`.
   149→
   150→## `hit merge` - Combining input files
   151→
   152→```
   153→hit merge -output outfile.i infile1.i infile2.i ...
   154→```
   155→
   156→will produce a single file `outfile.i` that is a merge of the `infile*.i` inputs
   157→(which would result in the same MOOSE simulation as the multiple input files).
   158→
   159→## `hit diff` - Highlighting differences between input files
   160→
   161→```
   162→hit diff left.i right.i
   163→```
   164→
   165→or
   166→
   167→```
   168→hit diff -left left1.i left2.i ... -right right1.i right2.i ...
   169→```
   170→
   171→performs a diff on the left and right files (in the second example merging all
   172→left and right files respectively first). This diff is not sensitive to order,
   173→formatting, or comments in the input files.
   174→
   175→## `hit common` - Extract common parameters between inputs
   176→
   177→```
   178→hit common file1.i file2.i ... > common_parameters.i
   179→```
   180→
   181→Will extract all parameters that are common to the specified input files (and
   182→have the same values). This can be used as the +first step+ in factoring out
   183→common settings into a single input file.
   184→
   185→## `hit subtract` - Removing common parameters parameters
   186→
   187→```
   188→hit subtract simulation_1_full.i common_parameters.i > simulation_1.i
   189→```
   190→
   191→removes the parameters in `common_parameters.i` from `simulation_1_full.i`,
   192→creating `simulation_1.i`. This is the +second step+ in factoring out common
   193→parameters from a set of input files. The resulting file can be run as
   194→`./mooseapp-opt -i common.i simulation_1.i` and will result in the same
   195→simulation as `./mooseapp-opt -i simulation_1_full.i`.
   196→

1→# Introduction to MOOSE
     2→
     3→!---
     4→
     5→# MOOSE Framework: Overview
     6→
     7→!row!
     8→!col! width=50%
     9→
    10→!media large_media/tutorials/darcy_thermo_mech/moose_intro.png
    11→
    12→!col-end!
    13→
    14→!col! width=50%
    15→
    16→- Developed by Idaho National Laboratory since 2008
    17→- Used for studying and analyzing nuclear reactor problems
    18→- Free and open source (LGPLv2 license)
    19→- Large user community (5000+ unique visitors/month)
    20→- Highly parallel and HPC capable
    21→- Developed and supported by full time INL staff - long-term support
    22→- https://www.mooseframework.inl.gov
    23→
    24→!col-end!
    25→!row-end!
    26→
    27→!---
    28→
    29→# MOOSE Framework: Key features
    30→
    31→!row!
    32→!col! width=50%
    33→
    34→!media large_media/tutorials/darcy_thermo_mech/moose_systems.png
    35→
    36→!col-end!
    37→
    38→!col! width=50%
    39→
    40→- Massively parallel computation - successfully run on >100,000 processor cores
    41→- Multiphysics solve capability - fully coupled and implicit solver
    42→- Multiscale solve capability - multiple applications can perform computation for a problem simultaneously
    43→- Provides high level interface to implement customized physics, geometries, boundary conditions, and material models
    44→- Initially developed to support nuclear R&D but now widely used for non-nuclear R&D also
    45→
    46→!col-end!
    47→!row-end!
    48→
    49→!---
    50→
    51→# MOOSE Framework: Core Philosophy
    52→
    53→- +Object-oriented design+ : Everything is an object with clear interfaces
    54→- +Modular architecture+ : Mix and match components and/or physics to achieve simulation goals
    55→- +Supporting Many Physics+ : Framework handles numerics, you focus on physics
    56→- +Dimension-independent+ : Run in 1D, 2D, or 3D with minimal changes to the input file
    57→- +Automatic differentiation+ : No need to compute Jacobians manually
    58→
    59→!---
    60→
    61→# MOOSE Framework: Applications
    62→
    63→!media large_media/tutorials/darcy_thermo_mech/moose_herd_2022.png style=width:100%;margin-left:auto;margin-right:auto;display:block;
    64→
    65→!---
    66→
    67→# MOOSE Systems Architecture
    68→
    69→!row!
    70→!col! width=40%
    71→
    72→+Core Systems:+
    73→
    74→- Mesh
    75→- Variables
    76→- Kernels
    77→- BCs
    78→- Materials
    79→- AuxKernels
    80→- Functions
    81→
    82→!col-end!
    83→
    84→!col! width=60%
    85→
    86→+Advanced Systems:+
    87→
    88→- MultiApps (multi-scale coupling)
    89→- Transfers (data exchange)
    90→- Postprocessors (scalar values)
    91→- VectorPostprocessors (vector values)
    92→- UserObjects (custom algorithms)
    93→- Controls (runtime parameter modification)
    94→- Executioner (solve control)
    95→
    96→!col-end!
    97→!row-end!
    98→
    99→- Each system has specific responsibilities
   100→- Systems interact through well-defined interfaces

1→#!/bin/bash
     2→
     3→REPO_DIR="$( cd "$( dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" )" && pwd )/../"
     4→hookfile="$REPO_DIR/.git/hooks/pre-commit"
     5→
     6→if [[ -f $hookfile ]]; then
     7→    echo "'$hookfile' already exists - aborting" 1>&2
     8→    exit 1
     9→fi
    10→
    11→cat > $hookfile << 'HOOKEOF'
    12→#!/bin/bash
    13→
    14→REPO_DIR="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"
    15→HIT="$REPO_DIR/moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit"
    16→
    17→# Check C++ formatting with clang-format
    18→patch=$(git clang-format --diff -- $(git diff --staged --name-only -- src include tests unit))
    19→if [[ "$patch" =~ "no modified files to format" || "$patch" =~ "clang-format did not modify any files" ]]; then
    20→    echo "" > /dev/null
    21→else
    22→    echo ""
    23→    echo "Your code is not properly formatted." >&2
    24→    echo "Run 'git clang-format' to resolve the following issues:" >&2
    25→    echo ""
    26→    echo "$patch"
    27→    exit 1
    28→fi
    29→
    30→# Check MOOSE input files with hit format
    31→if [[ ! -x "$HIT" ]]; then
    32→    echo "Warning: hit binary not found at $HIT, skipping .i file format check" >&2
    33→else
    34→    staged_i_files=$(git diff --staged --name-only -- '*.i')
    35→    if [[ -n "$staged_i_files" ]]; then
    36→        needs_format=false
    37→        while IFS= read -r f; do
    38→            [[ -f "$f" ]] || continue
    39→            tmpfile=$(mktemp)
    40→            git show ":$f" > "$tmpfile"
    41→            "$HIT" format "$tmpfile" 2>/dev/null
    42→            staged_content=$(git show ":$f")
    43→            formatted_content=$(cat "$tmpfile")
    44→            rm -f "$tmpfile"
    45→            if [[ "$staged_content" != "$formatted_content" ]]; then
    46→                echo "File '$f' needs hit formatting. Run: $HIT format $f" >&2
    47→                needs_format=true
    48→            fi
    49→        done <<< "$staged_i_files"
    50→        if $needs_format; then
    51→            echo "" >&2
    52→            echo "Run the above command(s) and re-stage the affected files." >&2
    53→            exit 1
    54→        fi
    55→    fi
    56→fi
    57→HOOKEOF
    58→
    59→chmod a+x $hookfile
    60→
    61→

## Summary of Search Results

I searched the TMAP8 repository at `/Users/bhavcv/projects/TMAP8/doc/content/` and the broader repository for markdown files and documentation related to pre-commit hooks, hit format, install-format-hook, and the agentic workflow.

### Files Found:

1. **AGENTS.md** (Root level documentation)
   - Path: `/Users/bhavcv/projects/TMAP8/AGENTS.md`
   - Contents: Comprehensive guidelines for AI agents working on TMAP8. Includes sections on:
     - Environment setup and pre-commit hook usage (mentions that pre-commit hooks handle all formatting and input file validation on every git commit)
     - References to `hit format` and validation with `hit check`
     - C++ code standards and testing requirements
     - Input file validation procedures
     - Session logging and GitHub issue requirements
     - Commit message requirements with trailers

2. **install-format-hook.sh** (Script file)
   - Path: `/Users/bhavcv/projects/TMAP8/scripts/install-format-hook.sh`
   - Contents: Bash script that:
     - Installs a pre-commit hook into `.git/hooks/pre-commit`
     - Validates C++ code formatting with `clang-format`
     - Validates MOOSE input files (`.i` files) using `hit format`
     - Provides user-friendly error messages if formatting issues are detected

3. **hit.md** (HIT format documentation)
   - Path: `/Users/bhavcv/projects/TMAP8/moose/framework/doc/content/hit.md`
   - Contents: Complete documentation of the `hit` command-line tool, including:
     - `hit format` subcommand for formatting input files with customizable styles
     - Other commands: find, merge, diff, common, subtract

4. **contributing.md** (Contribution guidelines)
   - Path: `/Users/bhavcv/projects/TMAP8/doc/content/getting_started/contributing.md`
   - Contents: Development workflow guide covering:
     - Code standards and documentation practices
     - Git workflow (forking, branching, committing)
     - Using `git clang-format` for code formatting
     - Documentation generation with MooseDocs

### Key Findings:

- **No dedicated scripts documentation page** exists in `/doc/content/`
- **No agentic workflow documentation page** exists in the main documentation
- The agentic workflow is documented in **AGENTS.md** (not in doc/content)
- Pre-commit hooks are installed via the **install-format-hook.sh** script and are mandatory for all commits
- **hit format** is the standard tool for formatting MOOSE input files and is integrated into the pre-commit hook
- The pre-commit hook ensures code consistency by checking both C++ formatting (clang-format) and input file formatting (hit format)

1→# Contributing to TMAP8
     2→
     3→TMAP8 is a collaborative software project, and all contributions to the code are welcome! Because
     4→multiple developers and groups are working on and using TMAP8, we have various standards, procedures,
     5→and testing processes to maintain the software quality of this project. Below, contribution guidelines
     6→and development best practices for TMAP8 are outlined.
     7→
     8→## Follow the TMAP8 code standards
     9→
    10→!style halign=left
    11→When modifying TMAP8, the development team requests that all [Code Standards](tmap8_scs.md) be
    12→followed. Additionally, if you are creating a new verification or validation case, make sure to follow the [V&V standards](/verification_and_validation_standards.md). These guidelines make sure that the repository code follows a consistent look and feel to
    13→make development easier.
    14→
    15→## Creating and Referencing Issues
    16→
    17→!style halign=left
    18→When finding an issue with the code, or developing/suggesting a new feature or enhancement, an issue
    19→should be created. This can be done ahead of time when developing code, or anytime before pushing
    20→your changes. In any case, you will need to list an issue number in one of your commits! To get started:
    21→
    22→1. Select “New issue” on this page: [https://github.com/idaholab/TMAP8/issues](https://github.com/idaholab/TMAP8/issues)
    23→1. Select either “bug report” or “feature request” (most should be feature requests)
    24→1. Provide issue descriptions of a couple of sentences (or as much as you see fit) following the prompts.
    25→1. Create issue
    26→
    27→You will now have an issue number next to the title. We will use it to link the pull request (PR) to the issue.
    28→
    29→## Work in a GitHub fork
    30→

file.content:
# Contributing to TMAP8

TMAP8 is a collaborative software project, and all contributions to the code are welcome! Because
multiple developers and groups are working on and using TMAP8, we have various standards, procedures,
and testing processes to maintain the software quality of this project. Below, contribution guidelines
and development best practices for TMAP8 are outlined.

## Follow the TMAP8 code standards

!style halign=left
When modifying TMAP8, the development team requests that all [Code Standards](tmap8_scs.md) be
followed. Additionally, if you are creating a new verification or validation case, make sure to follow the [V&V standards](/verification_and_validation_standards.md). These guidelines make sure that the repository code follows a consistent look and feel to
make development easier.

## Creating and Referencing Issues

!style halign=left
When finding an issue with the code, or developing/suggesting a new feature or enhancement, an issue
should be created. This can be done ahead of time when developing code, or anytime before pushing
your changes. In any case, you will need to list an issue number in one of your commits! To get started:

1. Select “New issue” on this page: [https://github.com/idaholab/TMAP8/issues](https://github.com/idaholab/TMAP8/issues)
1. Select either “bug report” or “feature request” (most should be feature requests)
1. Provide issue descriptions of a couple of sentences (or as much as you see fit) following the prompts.
1. Create issue

You will now have an issue number next to the title. We will use it to link the pull request (PR) to the issue.

## Work in a GitHub fork

!style halign=left
TMAP8 development follows a "Fork & Pull" development process. See more information on
[forking repositories in the GitHub documentation](https://guides.github.com/activities/forking).

### Fork TMAP8 and clone

1. Navigate to [the TMAP8 repository](https://github.com/idaholab/TMAP8)
1. Click the "Fork" button on the upper right of the code file tree to have a copy of TMAP8 in your own account
1. Clone your new fork to your local machine using the following command (this assumes you
   [have SSH set up on your GitHub account](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/adding-a-new-ssh-key-to-your-github-account)):

   ```
   cd ~/projects
   git clone git@github.com:<your_user_name>/tmap8.git
   ```

   In your local copy, this clone URL is given the shorthand name "origin". This will become relevant
   later in this document.

### Add an upstream git remote

!style halign=left
Add the main TMAP8 repository as an "upstream" git remote, so that you can
[git fetch](https://git-scm.org/docs/git-fetch) updates from the main repository:

```
cd ~/projects/TMAP8
git remote add upstream git@github.com:idaholab/TMAP8.git
```

To fetch changes, run:

```
git fetch upstream
```

## Creating a new branch

Before we make any changes locally (which would require having a working version of the application),
we will need to create a new branch.

1. In your terminal, go to your application:

   ```
   cd ~/projects/tmap8
   ```

1. It is usually best to make sure your changes are based on devel, so check it out for use:

   ```
   git checkout devel
   ```

1. Fetch and reset devel to get it up to date (make sure you do not have any changes on devel, because you would lose that):

   ```
   git fetch upstream && git reset --hard upstream/devel && git submodule update --init
   ```

1. Create a new branch, selecting an appropriate branch name for your development activities:

   ```
   git checkout -b <BRANCH_NAME>
   ```

## Make modifications and commit (the regular development workflow)

At this point, you have a local branch that is up to date with the upstream remote. You’re now ready
to make your changes. Remember, **small, consistent changes** are much better than dumping massive,
unrelated changes all at once.

1. Make your changes locally to address the issue (partly or fully) - using
   [VSCode is recommended](help/development/VSCode.md).
1. In your terminal, go to your application:

   ```
   cd ~/projects/tmap8
   ```

1. Make sure that the app still compiles and that the tests run:

   ```
   make -j6 && ./run_tests -j6
   ```

   If you modified the documentation, you can then build and test it locally:

   ```
   cd ~/projects/tmap8/doc && ./moosedocs.py build --serve
   ```

1. Type `git status` to see the status of your branch. That should show you the changed files and
   give you some commands to stage them.
1. Once you are ready, perform

   ```
   git add <FILE_NAME_1> <FILE_NAME_2> <FILE_NAME_3> ...
   ```

   to stage your updated files.
1. At that point, it is usually good practice to run

   ```
   git clang-format HEAD~1
   ```

   to fix the formatting of the files based on the `.clang-format` file in the root of the repository.
   If files are changed, run

   ```
   git add <...>
   ```

   for these files. Using `git status` again to check for changed files is helpful here.
1. Now you’re ready to commit. Run

   ```
   git commit
   ```

1. A file editor will appear. Now enter a short description of the commit.
   If you use VIM / VI as the default terminal editor, press the `i` key, and you’ll be able to edit the file.
   It is good practice to have a short title, and then a list of items describing the
   changes in more detail. At the bottom, you should add `(Ref. #<ISSUE_NUMBER>)`. It will look
   something like this:

   ```
   Updating this to enable that:
   - Update this file to do this
   - Update documentation to reflect that change
   - Create new test for new capability
   - Update existing gold files to reflect change

   (Ref. #1234)
   ```

1. To exit and save, press `esc`, then type `:wq` if you use VIM / VI as the editor. The commit file will close.
1. You might have to do several commits to capture all your changes.

## Generating new documentation pages using MooseDocs

During the course of development, especially during the creation of new TMAP8 objects (e.g., kernels,
boundary conditions, interface conditions, etc.), it is important to create documentation outlining
any new capabilities. Templates for object documents can be generated using the [MooseDocs system](MooseDocs/index.md)
using the "generate" sub-command. To generate templates for new objects, run:

```
cd ~/projects/tmap8
./moosedocs.py generate app_types TMAP8App
```

For example, with a new object called `TMAP8Diffusion` without documentation, the following output will be seen:

```
% ./moosedocs.py generate app_types TMAP8App
Creating/updating stub page: /Users/username/projects/tmap8/doc/content/source/kernels/TMAP8Diffusion.md
CRITICAL:0 ERROR:0 WARNING:0
```

And the following template would be created:

```markdown
# TMAP8Diffusion

!alert construction title=Undocumented Class
The TMAP8Diffusion has not been documented. The content listed below should be used as a starting point for
documenting the class, which includes the typical automatic documentation associated with a
MooseObject; however, what is contained is ultimately determined by what is necessary to make the
documentation clear for users.

!syntax description /Kernels/TMAP8Diffusion

## Overview

!! Replace these lines with information regarding the TMAP8Diffusion object.

## Example Input File Syntax

!! Describe and include an example of how to use the TMAP8Diffusion object.

!syntax parameters /Kernels/TMAP8Diffusion

!syntax inputs /Kernels/TMAP8Diffusion

!syntax children /Kernels/TMAP8Diffusion
```

Note that certain items, such as the source code description, object parameters, inputs in which the
object is used, and child objects are filled in automatically using the [MooseDocs/extensions/appsyntax.md].
The lines leading with `!!` as well as the `!alert` extension command should be removed and replaced
with relevant documentation regarding how to use the object.

!alert! note title=Use good documentation practices!
When creating documentation, it is particularly important to note any design limitations or assumptions
as well as best practices to apply when using the object.
!alert-end!

Again, make sure to commit these changes using the instructions above before pushing your branch.

## Pushing and submitting TMAP8 changes

At some point, you'll be ready to push your work to your fork and submit the changes as a pull request
(PR). To do this follow the following steps.

1. To push your work to your fork (remember, it has the remote name `origin`) run:

   ```
   git push origin <BRANCH_NAME>
   ```

1. A link will be printed in the terminal; visit that page.
1. There, you’ll find instructions to create your PR. Fill out the form (one can simply copy what is
   in the commit messages, or write a custom summary of your own) and submit your PR!
1. To notify others (or the TMAP8 development team), you can also tag people in your PR to get their
   attention.

#!/bin/bash

REPO_DIR="$( cd "$( dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" )" && pwd )/../"
hookfile="$REPO_DIR/.git/hooks/pre-commit"

if [[ -f $hookfile ]]; then
    echo "'$hookfile' already exists - aborting" 1>&2
    exit 1
fi

cat > $hookfile << 'HOOKEOF'
#!/bin/bash

REPO_DIR="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"
HIT="$REPO_DIR/moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit"

# Check C++ formatting with clang-format
patch=$(git clang-format --diff -- $(git diff --staged --name-only -- src include tests unit))
if [[ "$patch" =~ "no modified files to format" || "$patch" =~ "clang-format did not modify any files" ]]; then
    echo "" > /dev/null
else
    echo ""
    echo "Your code is not properly formatted." >&2
    echo "Run 'git clang-format' to resolve the following issues:" >&2
    echo ""
    echo "$patch"
    exit 1
fi

# Check MOOSE input files with hit format
if [[ ! -x "$HIT" ]]; then
    echo "Warning: hit binary not found at $HIT, skipping .i file format check" >&2
else
    staged_i_files=$(git diff --staged --name-only -- '*.i')
    if [[ -n "$staged_i_files" ]]; then
        needs_format=false
        while IFS= read -r f; do
            [[ -f "$f" ]] || continue
            tmpfile=$(mktemp)
            git show ":$f" > "$tmpfile"
            "$HIT" format "$tmpfile" 2>/dev/null
            staged_content=$(git show ":$f")
            formatted_content=$(cat "$tmpfile")
            rm -f "$tmpfile"
            if [[ "$staged_content" != "$formatted_content" ]]; then
                echo "File '$f' needs hit formatting. Run: $HIT format $f" >&2
                needs_format=true
            fi
        done <<< "$staged_i_files"
        if $needs_format; then
            echo "" >&2
            echo "Run the above command(s) and re-stage the affected files." >&2
            exit 1
        fi
    fi
fi
HOOKEOF

chmod a+x $hookfile

1. At that point, it is usually good practice to run

   ```
   git clang-format HEAD~1
   ```

   to fix the formatting of the files based on the `.clang-format` file in the root of the repository.
   If files are changed, run

   ```
   git add <...>
   ```

   for these files. Using `git status` again to check for changed files is helpful here.

## Installing the pre-commit formatting hook

TMAP8 provides a pre-commit hook that automatically enforces formatting on every `git commit`, covering both C++ source files and MOOSE input files. Install it once after cloning:

```
bash scripts/install-format-hook.sh
```

The hook performs two checks on every commit:

- **C++ formatting** — runs `git clang-format` on staged `.C` and `.h` files. If any file is not properly formatted, the commit is blocked and the diff is printed so you can run `git clang-format` to fix it.
- **MOOSE input file formatting** — runs `hit format` on every staged `.i` file. If any file differs from the canonical `hit` output, the commit is blocked and the offending file(s) are listed. To fix, run:

  ```
  moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit format <file>.i
  ```

  then re-stage the file and commit again.

!alert note title=Automated enforcement
Because the hook checks the staged content (not the working-tree copy), you must re-stage the file after formatting. Do not invoke `clang-format` or `hit format` manually on uncommitted changes — stage first, then let the hook tell you what needs fixing, or format and re-stage.

- **C++ formatting** — runs `git clang-format` on staged `.C` and `.h` files. If any file is not properly formatted, the commit is blocked and the diff is printed so you can run `git clang-format` to fix it.
- **MOOSE input file formatting** — runs `hit format` on every staged `.i` file. If any file differs from the canonical `hit` output, the commit is blocked and the offending file(s) are listed. To fix, run:

  ```
  moose/framework/contrib/hit/hit format <file>.i
  ```

  then re-stage the file and commit again.

!alert note title=Automated enforcement
Because the hook checks the staged content (not the working-tree copy), you must re-stage the file after formatting. Do not invoke `clang-format` or `hit format` manually on uncommitted changes — stage first, then let the hook tell you what needs fixing, or format and re-stage.
